critique.summary: The article claims that cryptocurrency industry super PACs spent $14.2 million in the Illinois primaries, with 90% of that spending failing to achieve its intended outcomes. It details that most of the funds were used to oppose Democratic candidates who ultimately won their primaries, resulting in ineffective spending.
mainPoints: Cryptocurrency super PACs spent $14.2 million in Illinois primaries.
90% ($12.8 million) of this spending was ineffective, supporting losing candidates or opposing winners.
The Fairshake pro-crypto super PAC network spent $10 million opposing Senate candidate Juliana Stratton and $2.5 million opposing La Shawn Ford, both of whom won their primaries.
The PACs only succeeded in races where outcomes were already highly likely.
This early spending represents less than 6% of the super PACs' total funds, indicating more spending to come.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the spending amounts and outcomes align with publicly available campaign finance data and election results from the Illinois primaries. Juliana Stratton and La Shawn Ford did win their primaries despite opposition spending, which supports the article's assertion of ineffective spending. However, the article does not cite specific sources or provide detailed data references, limiting full verification. The characterization of 90% spending as 'wasted' is an interpretation based on election outcomes but is consistent with the described spending patterns and results.
considerations: The article does not provide direct sources or links to campaign finance records or official election results.
It lacks perspectives from the super PACs or candidates involved, which could provide context on spending strategies or goals beyond winning primaries.
The article does not discuss potential long-term impacts of the spending beyond the primary election results.
The term 'wasted' is subjective and assumes the only objective was winning the primaries, without considering other possible strategic goals.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the ineffectiveness of crypto PAC spending in the Illinois primaries, with several commenters noting that money alone does not determine election outcomes. Some highlight broader political dynamics, including the influence of other PACs and demographic factors.

comments.author: tptacek
comments.text: Nobody's lobbying achieved objectives in the Illinois primary, which is more a statement about the ineffectiveness of lobbying (at least in these kinds of races) than anything else. The candidates that won were the candidates you'd expect to win given demographics and the recent political history of the region.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately points out that lobbying often fails to change election outcomes, especially in predictable demographic regions, reflecting a realistic understanding of political influence.

comments.author: BurningFrog
comments.text:
Fortunately, you can't typically "buy" elections by donating to campaigns.

Campaign spending does have an effect for unknown candidates, but once the voters know who you are and what you stand for, further spending doesn't move the needle.

It's true that the campaign with most money usually wins, but that does not the money caused the win!

One way to think about it is that the most popular candidate naturally gets the most donations, just like they get the most votes. It can also be a good investment to be on good terms with the future winner.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a nuanced view of campaign finance, distinguishing between effects on unknown versus established candidates, which aligns with political science research.

comments.author: mmahd7456
comments.text: Throwing money at a Republican primary candidate in Illinois is probably as ineffective as it would be in New York.  The big cities are just too deeply Democratic.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly notes the strong Democratic leanings in major urban areas, making Republican spending less effective, a well-known political reality.

comments.author: daft_pink
comments.text:
Pretty sure primary sending isn’t very helpful when it’s intended to change election results.

What’s helpful is donating to people who you already know are going to win so that they do you favors later on.
comments.analysis: The comment reflects common political strategy about focusing resources on probable winners rather than trying to flip races, which is a practical insight.

comments.author: jmyeet
comments.text:
You can't talk about what happened in the Illinois primaries without talking about the other PACs who spent big, specifically AIPAC and other dark-money Israel-affiliated PACs that spent to defeat pro-Palestinian candidates (eg Kat Abugazaleh) without ever once mentioning Israel [1].

It's far more accurate to say that pro-Zionist groups spent big in the Illinois primary and got mixed results. Crypto just went along for the ride.

There is a war in the Democratic Party between anti-genocide candidates, who enjoy 90% support in the base, and the establishment who is doing everything to defeat them, up to and including intentionally losing the 2024 presidential election [3].

Nobody cares about crypto.

[1]: https://www.politico.com/news/2026/03/18/aipac-israel-illino...

[2]: https://news.gallup.com/poll/702440/israelis-no-longer-ahead...

[3]: https://www.thenation.com/article/politics/dnc-autopsy-gaza-...
comments.analysis: While the comment introduces relevant context about other influential PACs, it mixes political analysis with emotional language and some unverified claims about intentions.

comments.author: lern_too_spel
comments.text: Good. I vote against anybody who is supported by crypto pacs as one of my top priorities in primaries. Unfortunately, Schiff beat Porter for the Senate seat in California, but happily the grifting Rishi Kumar continues to lose every seat he runs for.
comments.analysis: This comment is primarily emotional and expresses a personal voting stance without providing broader analytical insight or evidence.

comments.author: buddhistdude
comments.text:
"The cryptocurrency industry super PACs dumped $14.2 million into the Illinois primaries. 90% of that – $12.8 million – was wasted, in that it went to opposing Democratic candidates who won their primaries"

I read that as them having mistakenly sent the cryptos to the "opposing candidate"
comments.analysis: This comment summarizes the reported spending and outcome but may oversimplify the complex dynamics of campaign finance and election results.

comments.author: Arainach
comments.text: Is there a writeup of the objectives of lobbying/spending here?  Are specific bills/topics proposed for the upcoming session?
comments.analysis: This comment constructively seeks more detailed information about lobbying goals, indicating a desire for deeper understanding but does not provide analysis.
